_meta.authoringNotes: Distinct from hypotony-of-eye umbrella entry; focuses on idiopathic / occult presentations of chronic IOP 5 mmHg or below without overt cause. Sources cross-verified against AAO BCSC Glaucoma Section 10, Costa 2007 review (Surv Ophthalmol), Schubert 1996 review, and UBM-based occult cyclodialysis literature.

h1Title: Primary Ocular Hypotony: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Primary ocular hypotony is sustained intraocular pressure of 5 mmHg or lower with no identifiable cause on standard ophthalmic workup, in an eye without recent surgery, trauma, or known inflammation. It is rare, accounting for fewer than 1 in 10,000 ophthalmology referrals, and most cases reclassify as secondary once dedicated imaging (UBM, anterior-segment OCT) and a full uveitis panel uncover an occult cyclodialysis cleft, chronic low-grade uveitis with ciliary shutdown, or ciliary body atrophy. The presenting complaint is usually painless blurred central vision from hypotony maculopathy with chorioretinal folds on OCT. Treatment hunts for the hidden cause; truly idiopathic disease responds to atropine, oral midodrine, viscoelastic, and selective surgery.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated below 0.01% of the general population; below 1% of all hypotony referrals after workup remain truly idiopathic
keyStats.demographics: Wide age range; most patients are middle-aged adults; no strong sex predominance
keyStats.avgAge: Mean presentation age 45-60 in published case series
keyStats.globalCases: Truly idiopathic cases number in the low thousands worldwide; most series report fewer than 50 cases per institution

definition: Primary ocular hypotony (ICD-10: H44.41) is a chronic state of intraocular pressure at or below 5 mmHg, persisting on at least two consecutive Goldmann applanation readings separated by a week or more, in an eye that has no documented penetrating surgery, glaucoma drainage device, recent trauma, overt anterior or posterior segment inflammation, retinal detachment, or systemic disease known to lower IOP. The hypotony is therefore unexplained at initial workup. Pathophysiology centers on the ciliary body: aqueous humor production by the non-pigmented ciliary epithelium falls below the rate of uveoscleral and trabecular outflow, or a microscopic separation of the ciliary body from the scleral spur (occult cyclodialysis cleft) bypasses the conventional outflow pathway entirely. After dedicated imaging such as ultrasound biomicroscopy, high-resolution anterior-segment OCT, and a full uveitis panel, most cases reclassify into a secondary etiology, and the residual idiopathic group is small. The condition is distinct from the umbrella term ocular hypotony, which includes all causes of low IOP including post-filtration surgery, post-trauma, and overt uveitis. It is managed by glaucoma-trained ophthalmologists and, when retinal complications dominate, in collaboration with vitreoretinal surgeons.

typesClassification.description: Chronic IOP 5 mmHg or below with no cause identifiable on UBM, anterior-segment OCT, full uveitis workup, gonioscopy, and B-scan ultrasound. Accounts for fewer than 1 in 5 cases initially labeled primary.

A microscopic separation of the ciliary body from the scleral spur missed on routine gonioscopy and only visible on UBM or high-resolution AS-OCT. The most common single cause uncovered in supposedly idiopathic series.

typesClassification.type: Occult chronic uveitis with ciliary shutdown
typesClassification.description: Low-grade, clinically silent inflammation of the ciliary body that suppresses aqueous production. Found on flare metering, dedicated FA/ICG, or anterior-chamber paracentesis cytology after extended workup.

Idiopathic structural thinning of the ciliary processes or congenital underdevelopment, occasionally familial. Confirmed by UBM showing reduced ciliary body thickness without overt inflammation or cleft.

primarySymptoms: Painless gradual blurring of central vision, often beginning unilaterally and progressing over weeks to months as hypotony maculopathy develops.
Photopsias or flashes of light reported in roughly 30-40% of patients, related to vitreoretinal traction along chorioretinal folds.
Mild ocular ache or a soft, foggy sensation in the eye, distinct from the sharp pain of acute angle closure or scleritis.
Reduced visual acuity ranging from 20/30 to 20/200 depending on severity of macular involvement and chronicity.
Decreased contrast sensitivity and metamorphopsia as chorioretinal folds distort the foveal contour.
Slightly soft globe on gentle palpation noted by experienced clinicians, with the eye feeling doughy compared to the fellow eye.
Shallowing or deepening of the anterior chamber depending on whether choroidal effusions are present or whether the ciliary body has receded posteriorly.
Mild conjunctival injection in some patients despite the absence of overt inflammation on slit-lamp examination.
Subtle hyperopic shift on refraction of 1.0-2.5 diopters as the axial length shortens with chronic hypotony.
Absence of headache, photophobia, or systemic symptoms in chronic primary disease, which is typically painless.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent IOP of 5 mmHg or lower on two routine ophthalmic visits in an otherwise healthy eye
Unexplained drop of 1.0-2.5 diopters of refractive error toward hyperopia without lens change
New onset of mild metamorphopsia or central blur in one eye with no obvious cause on fundus exam
Faint chorioretinal folds visible on macular OCT in an eye reading below 8 mmHg
emergencySigns: Sudden drop in vision to counting fingers or worse, which may indicate large choroidal effusion, suprachoroidal hemorrhage, or rapidly progressive maculopathy requiring same-day vitreoretinal review
New severe eye pain, redness, and photophobia, which may signal acute uveitis flare, endophthalmitis, or phthisis transition
Visible globe collapse or marked softening with rapid hyperopic shift, suggesting risk of imminent phthisis bulbi
Pupil distortion, hyphema, or new red reflex change, suggesting previously undetected anterior segment pathology requiring urgent gonioscopy and UBM

causes.description: A microscopic separation of the longitudinal ciliary muscle from the scleral spur creates a direct conduit from the anterior chamber to the suprachoroidal space. Often results from forgotten low-energy trauma, vigorous eye rubbing, or surgical instrumentation years earlier. Identified on UBM in 30-40% of supposedly primary cases.

causes.cause: Subclinical chronic uveitis with ciliary shutdown
causes.description: Persistent low-grade inflammation of the ciliary body (often associated with sarcoidosis, Fuchs heterochromic iridocyclitis, or HLA-B27 disease) suppresses aqueous production. Anterior chamber may show only 1+ cell or be entirely quiet on slit lamp; laser flare metering or anterior chamber paracentesis cytology reveals the inflammation.

causes.cause: Ciliary body atrophy of unknown origin
causes.description: Spontaneous degeneration of the ciliary processes with reduced non-pigmented epithelial function. UBM shows a thinned ciliary body without effusion or cleft. The underlying trigger is rarely identified in living patients; histopathology in enucleated eyes shows fibrosis and apoptosis of the bilayered epithelium.

An anterior segment dysgenesis variant in which the ciliary body fails to develop fully. Often presents in younger adults with longstanding mild hypotony that becomes symptomatic when maculopathy develops. May be sporadic or part of syndromic conditions (Axenfeld-Rieger, Peters anomaly variants).

Very rare autosomal dominant or recessive disorders affecting aqueous production genes. Family history of low IOP, occasionally with mild anterior segment dysgenesis, supports the diagnosis. Genetic causes are still being characterized.

causes.cause: Undetected systemic hypotension or autonomic dysfunction
causes.description: Chronic systemic hypotension lowers episcleral venous pressure and ciliary perfusion, reducing aqueous production. Patients with dysautonomia, severe weight loss, or chronic adrenal insufficiency may present with low IOP that resolves when systemic pressure normalizes.

riskFactors.description: Low-energy blunt injury years earlier can produce a small cyclodialysis cleft that goes unnoticed until hypotony maculopathy develops. Detailed history-taking and UBM identify these in many idiopathic cases.

riskFactors.factor: Personal history of autoimmune or granulomatous disease
riskFactors.description: Sarcoidosis, HLA-B27-related disease, syphilis, and tuberculosis can produce subclinical chronic uveitis with ciliary shutdown. A baseline uveitis panel is standard in any primary hypotony workup.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of low IOP or anterior segment dysgenesis
riskFactors.description: Suggests a familial primary hypotony syndrome or congenital ciliary hypoplasia. Genetic counseling and screening of first-degree relatives can be appropriate when a clear pattern emerges.
Sustained systolic blood pressure under 90 mmHg, postural autonomic failure, or adrenal insufficiency reduces ciliary perfusion. Treating the underlying systemic condition can restore IOP.
Long axial length and thinner sclera predispose to hypotony maculopathy at relatively mild IOP reductions. Hypotony maculopathy can appear at IOPs of 6-8 mmHg in highly myopic eyes.

riskFactors.factor: History of intraocular surgery without obvious filtration
riskFactors.description: Even routine phacoemulsification or vitrectomy can leave subtle ciliary dysfunction or microscopic clefts; reviewing prior op notes and imaging is essential before labeling hypotony as primary.

riskFactors.factor: Severe weight loss or chronic illness
riskFactors.description: Cachexia, dehydration, and chronic adrenal insufficiency are associated with episcleral venous pressure drops and reduced aqueous production.

affectedDemographics: Most published case series originate from tertiary glaucoma referral centers; community prevalence is unknown
Reported in adults across the third through seventh decades; pediatric cases are typically congenital ciliary hypoplasia
No clear sex predominance in the largest review series (Costa & Arcieri 2007)
Highly myopic eyes are over-represented, likely because hypotony maculopathy becomes symptomatic at higher IOP levels in long eyes
More frequently diagnosed in regions with widespread access to UBM and AS-OCT, suggesting many cases remain unrecognized elsewhere
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis follows a structured exclusion pathway. Goldmann applanation tonometry confirms IOP at or below 5 mmHg on at least two visits a week or more apart; pneumotonometry or rebound tonometry should not substitute for Goldmann at these low pressures because of measurement error. Slit-lamp examination assesses anterior chamber depth, looks for keratic precipitates or flare, and evaluates lens position. Gonioscopy is performed under low light with minimal pressure to look for a visible cyclodialysis cleft; routine gonioscopy misses up to 50% of clefts, so ultrasound biomicroscopy (UBM) or high-resolution anterior-segment OCT is mandatory. Posterior segment evaluation includes dilated fundus examination, B-scan ultrasound for choroidal effusion or thickening, and macular OCT to detect chorioretinal folds and inner retinal wrinkling that define hypotony maculopathy. A full uveitis workup is standard in any primary hypotony case and includes chest X-ray, serum ACE and lysozyme, FTA-ABS or RPR for syphilis, QuantiFERON or PPD for tuberculosis, ANA, HLA-B27, and CBC with ESR. Laser flare photometry, where available, can detect subclinical anterior chamber inflammation that slit-lamp examination misses. Where the workup remains negative, repeat imaging at 6 and 12 months and a careful family history complete the exclusion of secondary causes. Truly idiopathic primary hypotony is a diagnosis of exclusion reached only after this protocol.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The reference standard for measuring IOP. Confirms hypotony at 5 mmHg or below on at least two visits separated by 7 or more days. Avoids the systematic underestimation that non-contact and rebound tonometers show at low pressures.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Topical anesthetic and fluorescein are instilled. The patient rests their chin on the slit lamp while the examiner applies the tonometer prism for a few seconds. Painless and takes under a minute per eye.

diagnosticTests.purpose: High-frequency ultrasound (50 MHz) images the ciliary body, anterior chamber angle, and pars plana at resolutions invisible on routine gonioscopy. Detects occult cyclodialysis clefts, ciliary body atrophy, and pars plana cysts in 30-40% of supposedly primary hypotony cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed lying flat or seated. A water-filled eyecup is placed over the anesthetized eye and the probe is moved gently around it. Scan takes 10-15 minutes per eye and is painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects hypotony maculopathy by visualizing chorioretinal folds radiating from the fovea and inner retinal corrugations. Quantifies central foveal thickness and choroidal thickness, which both increase in hypotony.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-contact infrared imaging performed at the slit lamp. The patient fixates on an internal target while the scan is captured. Takes 1-2 minutes per eye, no pain or pupil dilation strictly required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects choroidal effusion or thickening, vitreous opacities, and posterior scleral changes that point toward inflammatory or structural causes. Especially useful when the fundus view is limited.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A water-based gel is applied to the closed eyelid and a small probe is moved across it. Lasts 5-10 minutes per eye and is painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rules out subclinical infectious and autoimmune causes that produce chronic ciliary shutdown. Standard panel: serum ACE and lysozyme (sarcoidosis), FTA-ABS or RPR (syphilis), QuantiFERON-TB Gold (tuberculosis), ANA, HLA-B27, CBC, ESR, and chest imaging.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood draw plus a chest X-ray or low-dose CT. Results typically available within 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Complements UBM by providing high-resolution cross-sectional imaging of the angle, ciliary body face, and anterior chamber. Useful for detecting subtle cyclodialysis clefts when UBM is equivocal.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-contact imaging at the slit lamp; the patient looks at a fixation target while the scan is captured in seconds. No pupil dilation required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies anterior chamber protein in photons per millisecond, detecting subclinical inflammation invisible on slit-lamp examination. Helps identify occult chronic uveitis as a primary hypotony driver.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed at a specialized instrument; the patient rests their chin and the laser beam is automatically directed into the anterior chamber. Each measurement takes seconds and is painless.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of primary ocular hypotony begins with continued search for the hidden cause, since most cases reclassify with extended workup. Empirical management is started in parallel when vision-threatening maculopathy is present. Topical atropine 1% three times daily relaxes the ciliary muscle, deepens the anterior chamber, and increases aqueous production through cycloplegia; it is the first-line pharmacologic step and improves IOP by 2-4 mmHg in roughly half of patients. Oral midodrine 5-10 mg three times daily raises systemic and episcleral venous pressure, modestly increasing IOP in patients with documented systemic hypotension or autonomic dysfunction. Oral pseudoephedrine has been used historically with similar mechanism but is less favored due to cardiovascular side effects. Topical and systemic corticosteroids are appropriate when laser flare photometry, anterior chamber paracentesis, or serology suggests subclinical uveitis; topical prednisolone acetate 1% every two hours initially with a slow taper, combined with cycloplegia, can reverse hypotony when ciliary shutdown is the cause. Viscoelastic injection into the anterior chamber (sodium hyaluronate 1%) physically reforms the chamber and provides a temporary IOP boost while definitive therapy is planned. When a cyclodialysis cleft is identified on imaging, direct cyclopexy under gonioscopic guidance, laser photocoagulation of the cleft, or transscleral cryotherapy closes the abnormal pathway and resolves hypotony in 70-85% of cases. Pars plana vitrectomy with gas tamponade and internal scleral buckling has been reported for refractory chronic hypotony unresponsive to medical and standard surgical approaches, with success rates around 60% in small series. The aim is preservation of vision and prevention of phthisis bulbi; truly idiopathic disease may require multiple sequential interventions over months to years.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical atropine 1% three times daily
medicalTreatments.description: Long-acting cycloplegic that relaxes the ciliary muscle, deepens the anterior chamber, and pulls the ciliary body forward toward the scleral spur. First-line pharmacologic agent in primary hypotony.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest IOP rise of 2-4 mmHg in about half of treated eyes; superior when an occult cyclodialysis cleft is present, where mechanical re-apposition can close the cleft.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral midodrine 5-10 mg three times daily
medicalTreatments.description: Peripheral alpha-1 agonist that raises systemic blood pressure and episcleral venous pressure, reducing aqueous outflow and modestly raising IOP. Particularly useful in patients with documented systemic hypotension.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mean IOP rise of 1-3 mmHg in small series; greatest effect in patients with baseline systolic pressure under 100 mmHg.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical prednisolone acetate 1% with cycloplegia
medicalTreatments.description: When subclinical uveitis is suspected on flare metering or serology, intensive topical steroid (every 1-2 hours initially) combined with atropine treats ciliary inflammation and restores aqueous production.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolution of hypotony in 60-75% of cases where occult inflammation is the cause; failure rate higher when ciliary atrophy has already developed.

medicalTreatments.name: Intracameral viscoelastic injection (sodium hyaluronate 1%)
medicalTreatments.description: Temporary reformation of the anterior chamber with cohesive viscoelastic. Used to provide a short-term IOP boost while definitive therapy is arranged, or to allow chorioretinal folds to flatten before surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective short-term (24-72 hours); persistent IOP rise occurs in 30-40% of treated eyes.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral pseudoephedrine 30-60 mg three times daily (selected cases)
medicalTreatments.description: Older option with mechanism similar to midodrine. Use limited by cardiovascular side effects (hypertension, tachycardia, insomnia). Reserved for patients who cannot tolerate midodrine.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable to midodrine in small series; side-effect profile makes it second-line.

surgicalOptions.name: Direct cyclopexy for cyclodialysis cleft
surgicalOptions.description: Open surgical reattachment of the ciliary body to the scleral spur, typically via a partial-thickness scleral flap and direct suturing under gonioscopic visualization. Performed when imaging confirms a cleft that has failed conservative therapy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 70-85% closure of cleft with IOP normalization in published series; risk of postoperative IOP spike requires monitoring.

surgicalOptions.name: Argon laser photocoagulation of cyclodialysis cleft
surgicalOptions.description: Targeted endolaser or transscleral laser application to the edge of the cleft induces inflammation and adhesion. Less invasive than direct cyclopexy; appropriate for small clefts under 60 degrees.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 50-70% success at first treatment; repeat treatment increases overall success.

surgicalOptions.name: Pars plana vitrectomy with gas tamponade
surgicalOptions.description: Vitrectomy with internal tamponade (SF6 or C3F8 gas) re-pressurizes the eye and flattens chorioretinal folds. Considered in chronic refractory hypotony with maculopathy that does not respond to medical or cleft-directed therapy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Roughly 60% sustained IOP recovery in small series; vision improvement is variable.

surgicalOptions.name: Transscleral cryotherapy or diathermy to the ciliary body
surgicalOptions.description: Limited indications, used when the goal is to provoke ciliary body inflammation and adhesion. Rare in primary hypotony; more often used for cyclodialysis cleft when laser fails.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reported closure of cleft in 50-65% of cases; risk of secondary phthisis or chronic inflammation limits use.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary salt and fluid loading
alternativeTreatments.description: In patients with systemic hypotension or autonomic dysfunction, increased oral salt (3-6 g daily) and water intake raise systemic and episcleral venous pressure, modestly supporting IOP. Adjunctive only and contraindicated in heart failure or uncontrolled hypertension.

alternativeTreatments.name: Compression stockings and abdominal binders
alternativeTreatments.description: Reduce venous pooling in dysautonomia and modestly raise systemic blood pressure, indirectly supporting episcleral venous pressure. Useful supportive measure in patients with autonomic-related primary hypotony.

whySeeSpecialist: Primary ocular hypotony is rare and time-sensitive: progressive hypotony maculopathy and chorioretinal folds become permanent if untreated for more than 6-12 months. A glaucoma-trained ophthalmologist with access to UBM, AS-OCT, and laser flare photometry is best placed to perform the systematic exclusion of secondary causes and to coordinate surgical input from a vitreoretinal colleague when maculopathy is present. General ophthalmology should refer any patient with two confirmed Goldmann IOP readings of 5 mmHg or lower and no obvious surgical or traumatic cause.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a glaucoma subspecialist who routinely performs UBM and high-resolution AS-OCT in their own clinic and has documented experience managing cyclodialysis clefts. Ask whether the practice has access to laser flare photometry and a uveitis specialist; both are essential for excluding subclinical inflammation. For surgical management, a center that performs combined glaucoma and vitreoretinal surgery is preferable, since refractory cases may need both cyclopexy and vitrectomy. Continuity of care matters because medical management often takes months before surgical decisions are made.
preventionStrategies: Use protective eyewear during sports and high-risk occupations to prevent low-energy ocular trauma that can cause subtle cyclodialysis clefts
Promptly evaluate any persistent IOP reading below 8 mmHg in adults with unexplained vision change
Maintain systemic blood pressure within a normal range when feasible; treat autonomic dysfunction and adrenal insufficiency
Screen first-degree relatives for low IOP when a familial primary hypotony pattern is suspected
Avoid vigorous chronic eye rubbing, which can extend a subclinical cyclodialysis cleft
lifestyleModifications: Avoid vigorous eye rubbing, which can extend a subclinical cyclodialysis cleft
Wear polycarbonate protective lenses during contact sports or high-risk work tasks
Maintain adequate hydration and salt intake within cardiovascular limits to support systemic and episcleral venous pressure
Attend regular follow-up every 3-6 months once primary hypotony is diagnosed, even when stable
Avoid Valsalva-heavy strenuous activity in the first 6 weeks after cyclopexy or vitrectomy

recommended: Adequate daily salt intake (3-6 g sodium daily) for patients with documented systemic hypotension
Sufficient fluid intake (1.5-2 L water daily unless restricted) to support intravascular volume
Balanced diet rich in B12, iron, and folate to address any anemia contributing to systemic hypotension
Caffeine in moderation can transiently raise blood pressure and may help in dysautonomia
avoid: Severe calorie restriction or fasting that worsens systemic hypotension
Excessive alcohol consumption, which vasodilates and lowers systemic pressure
High-potassium loads in patients also taking midodrine, as electrolyte shifts can blunt the effect
Dehydration during exercise or hot weather

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise is generally safe and may help patients with autonomic-related hypotony by improving baroreflex function. Avoid Valsalva-heavy resistance training and inversions (yoga headstands, heavy weightlifting) in eyes with active maculopathy or recent surgery, as transient IOP swings can extend chorioretinal folds. Resume normal exercise 4-6 weeks after surgical intervention with ophthalmologist approval.
dailyManagement: Instill prescribed atropine drops at the same times each day; do not skip doses, as the cycloplegic effect requires steady levels
Record any new flashes, floaters, or sudden vision change and report them within 24 hours
Attend follow-up visits at 4-6 weeks initially, then every 3 months once stable
Bring all prior ophthalmic records and imaging to each visit so the specialist can track ciliary body changes
Monitor home blood pressure once daily if also on midodrine to avoid supine hypertension
prognosis: Prognosis depends on whether a hidden cause is found. In published series, 50-70% of patients initially labeled primary improve substantially when an occult cyclodialysis cleft or subclinical uveitis is uncovered and treated directly; vision recovers to 20/40 or better in two-thirds of these reclassified cases when chorioretinal folds are flattened within 6-12 months of onset. Truly idiopathic primary hypotony has a worse outlook: roughly 30% of patients progress to chronic hypotony maculopathy with permanent metamorphopsia and reduced acuity, and a smaller fraction proceeds to phthisis bulbi over 5-10 years despite multiple interventions. Early recognition is the single strongest predictor of visual outcome; eyes diagnosed within 6 months of hypotony onset have visual acuity outcomes roughly two Snellen lines better than those diagnosed after 1 year. Lifelong follow-up is required because hypotony can recur after initially successful treatment, and the fellow eye should be monitored when a familial component is suspected.
recoveryTimeline: After topical atropine and midodrine initiation, IOP response is typically seen within 2-4 weeks. When subclinical uveitis is treated with topical steroids and cycloplegia, IOP and chorioretinal folds may take 2-3 months to normalize. Surgical cyclopexy produces immediate IOP rise within days, with maculopathy resolving over 1-3 months as the eye reforms. Refractive shift toward baseline (away from hyperopia) lags behind IOP recovery and may continue improving for 6-12 months. Visual acuity recovery plateaus by 6 months in most patients.
complications: Chronic hypotony maculopathy with permanent chorioretinal folds and metamorphopsia, occurring in 30-40% of untreated cases beyond 12 months
Phthisis bulbi (shrunken non-functional eye) in roughly 5-10% of long-standing truly idiopathic disease
Choroidal effusion or detachment, which can become recurrent and may require drainage
Cataract progression accelerated by chronic low pressure and any required surgery
Suprachoroidal hemorrhage during surgical intervention on a chronically hypotonous eye
Iatrogenic IOP spike after successful cyclopexy or laser treatment, requiring temporary topical pressure-lowering therapy
Visual loss from optic nerve dysfunction in advanced phthisis

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology EyeWiki: Ocular Hypotony
supportResources.description: Peer-reviewed clinician and patient resource covering classification, workup, and management of all forms of ocular hypotony, including primary and idiopathic presentations.

supportResources.name: American Glaucoma Society Patient Information
supportResources.description: Patient-facing materials from the leading US glaucoma specialty society, including information on intraocular pressure and related disorders.

supportResources.name: Royal College of Ophthalmologists Patient Information
supportResources.description: UK specialist-society patient leaflets covering eye-pressure problems, glaucoma, and related conditions.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ocular Hypotony (umbrella overview)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: The umbrella term covers all causes of low IOP including post-surgical wound leak, post-trauma, drainage devices, and overt uveitis. Primary ocular hypotony is the narrow subgroup with no identifiable cause on standard workup.

confusedWithConditions.name: Glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Glaucoma is defined by elevated IOP and progressive optic-nerve damage; primary hypotony is the opposite pressure problem. Treatment goals (lowering versus raising IOP) are mirror-image, and the conditions almost never coexist in the same eye.

confusedWithConditions.name: Normal-Pressure Glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Normal-pressure glaucoma has IOP within the statistical normal range (10-21 mmHg) with optic-nerve damage. Primary hypotony has IOP below 5 mmHg without nerve damage; the conditions are not pressure-equivalent.

confusedWithConditions.name: Age-Related Macular Degeneration
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: AMD produces central vision loss with drusen, RPE changes, or neovascular leakage on OCT; primary hypotony produces central vision loss from chorioretinal folds with normal RPE and drusen-free macula. IOP measurement separates the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Flat Anterior Chamber with Hypotony
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Flat anterior chamber hypotony has a clear mechanical cause, typically wound leak or choroidal effusion, with iris-cornea touch on slit lamp. Primary hypotony preserves anterior chamber depth (often deep, not flat) and has no identifiable mechanism.

relatedConditions.name: Ocular Hypotony
relatedConditions.relevance: Parent category. Primary ocular hypotony is the idiopathic subgroup within the broader hypotony spectrum; readers comparing both pages get the umbrella overview alongside the narrow primary diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: Flat Anterior Chamber Hypotony
relatedConditions.relevance: Sibling diagnosis in the hypotony family with overt mechanical findings; understanding the contrast clarifies why primary hypotony is diagnosed by exclusion.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Opposite-pressure condition sharing the same anatomical pathway (ciliary body and trabecular outflow); useful comparison for patients trying to understand intraocular pressure.

relatedConditions.name: Normal-Pressure Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Demonstrates that IOP and optic-nerve health are dissociable; helps patients understand why low IOP can be pathological.

relatedConditions.name: Age-Related Macular Degeneration
relatedConditions.relevance: Common competing cause of central vision loss in older adults; differentiation rests on IOP and OCT findings.

faqs.question: What is primary ocular hypotony?
faqs.answer: Primary ocular hypotony is intraocular pressure of 5 mmHg or lower with no identifiable cause on standard workup. It must persist on two Goldmann readings a week apart, in an eye without recent surgery, trauma, or known inflammation. Most cases reclassify once imaging finds a hidden cause.

faqs.question: How is primary ocular hypotony different from the general hypotony of eye page?
faqs.answer: The general hypotony page covers all causes including wound leak, drainage device over-filtration, trauma, and overt uveitis. Primary ocular hypotony is the narrow idiopathic subgroup with no cause on standard workup. Both share low IOP but differ in workup and treatment.

faqs.question: What IOP level defines hypotony?
faqs.answer: Statistical hypotony is intraocular pressure below 6.5 mmHg, or more than 3 standard deviations below the population mean of 15 mmHg. Clinical hypotony is any IOP that produces structural or visual consequences, typically 5 mmHg or below, but can occur at higher pressures in long myopic eyes.

faqs.question: What symptoms does primary ocular hypotony cause?
faqs.answer: The main symptom is gradual painless central blurring from hypotony maculopathy, where chorioretinal folds distort the fovea. Patients may also notice mild metamorphopsia, photopsias, a hyperopic shift, and a soft sensation in the eye. Pain is usually absent in chronic disease.

faqs.question: How is the diagnosis confirmed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires Goldmann tonometry showing IOP at or below 5 mmHg on two visits a week apart, plus exclusion of secondary causes. UBM looks for an occult cleft, macular OCT detects chorioretinal folds, B-scan checks for choroidal effusion, and serology excludes subclinical uveitis.

faqs.question: Why is ultrasound biomicroscopy so important?
faqs.answer: Ultrasound biomicroscopy images the ciliary body and angle at higher resolution than gonioscopy. It identifies occult cyclodialysis clefts in 30-40% of supposedly primary hypotony cases, allowing many patients to be reclassified and treated with cleft-directed therapy.

faqs.question: What causes most cases that look primary at first?
faqs.answer: On extended workup, most cases initially labeled primary turn out to have an occult cyclodialysis cleft, subclinical chronic uveitis with ciliary shutdown, or ciliary body atrophy. Truly idiopathic disease without any identifiable cause is rare and is a diagnosis of exclusion.

faqs.question: How is primary ocular hypotony treated?
faqs.answer: First-line is topical atropine 1% three times daily to relax the ciliary body. Oral midodrine 5-10 mg three times daily raises systemic and episcleral venous pressure. Topical steroids treat subclinical uveitis. Viscoelastic injection and cyclopexy are reserved for refractory cases.

faqs.question: Does atropine really raise IOP?
faqs.answer: Atropine 1% three times daily produces a modest IOP rise of 2-4 mmHg in about half of treated eyes with primary hypotony. The mechanism is ciliary muscle relaxation that deepens the anterior chamber and may re-appose the ciliary body to the scleral spur, closing small cyclodialysis clefts.

faqs.question: What is hypotony maculopathy?
faqs.answer: Hypotony maculopathy is the retinal complication of chronic low IOP, with chorioretinal folds radiating from the fovea and distorting central vision. OCT shows wrinkled retinal layers and corrugated choroid. Early treatment reverses the folds; chronic disease leaves permanent metamorphopsia.

faqs.question: Can primary ocular hypotony lead to permanent vision loss?
faqs.answer: Yes. If untreated for more than 12 months, chorioretinal folds become permanent and central vision can drop to 20/100 or worse. Severe long-standing cases progress to phthisis bulbi in 5-10% of truly idiopathic patients. Diagnosis within 6 months gives the best chance of full recovery.

faqs.question: Is surgery always needed?
faqs.answer: No. Most patients respond to topical atropine, oral midodrine, and treatment of any uncovered subclinical uveitis. Surgery (cyclopexy, laser cleft closure, or vitrectomy with gas tamponade) is reserved for documented clefts that fail medical therapy or chronic hypotony with progressive maculopathy.

faqs.question: How successful is cyclopexy for an occult cleft?
faqs.answer: Direct surgical cyclopexy closes the cyclodialysis cleft and normalizes IOP in 70-85% of patients in published series. Laser photocoagulation closes 50-70% of small clefts at first treatment, with success rising on repeat. Both approaches can produce a transient postoperative IOP spike.

faqs.question: What is the difference between primary hypotony and normal-pressure glaucoma?
faqs.answer: Normal-pressure glaucoma has IOP within the statistical normal range (10-21 mmHg) but with progressive optic-nerve damage. Primary ocular hypotony has IOP below 5 mmHg without nerve damage. The conditions sit at opposite ends of the pressure spectrum and need different treatments.

faqs.question: Should family members be screened?
faqs.answer: Family screening is reasonable when there is a clear pattern of low IOP across generations or anterior segment dysgenesis. A baseline Goldmann reading in each first-degree relative is a low-cost first step; genetic testing for familial hypotony genes remains research-grade in most centers.

faqs.question: Can the condition recur after successful treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cyclodialysis clefts can reopen, ciliary atrophy can progress, and subclinical uveitis can flare. Lifelong follow-up every 3-6 months catches recurrence early. Patients should report any new blurred vision or hyperopic refractive shift between scheduled visits.

faqs.question: Is midodrine safe for long-term use?
faqs.answer: Midodrine is generally well tolerated; main risks are supine hypertension, urinary retention, and scalp tingling. Daily home blood pressure monitoring is recommended, and the last dose should be at least 4 hours before bedtime to reduce supine hypertension. Annual review of need is appropriate.

faqs.question: Why was my uveitis blood panel ordered if my eye looks quiet?
faqs.answer: Subclinical chronic uveitis is a leading hidden cause of primary ocular hypotony. The eye can look quiet on slit-lamp examination while the ciliary body is suppressed by inflammation. Serology for sarcoidosis, syphilis, TB, and HLA-B27 disease catches diagnoses that would otherwise be missed.

faqs.question: Can systemic low blood pressure affect my eye pressure?
faqs.answer: Yes, in selected patients. Chronic systemic hypotension and autonomic dysfunction lower episcleral venous pressure and ciliary perfusion, reducing aqueous production. Treating the systemic problem with salt, fluid, compression garments, or midodrine can restore IOP in this subgroup.

faqs.question: How often do I need follow-up?
faqs.answer: After diagnosis, follow-up at 4-6 weeks initially confirms response to therapy. Once stable, visits every 3 months for the first year and then every 6 months catch recurrence and monitor maculopathy. Macular OCT is repeated at each visit to track chorioretinal folds.

faqs.question: Does primary hypotony affect contact lens or glasses wear?
faqs.answer: A hyperopic refractive shift of 1.0-2.5 diopters is common in chronic hypotony, so prescriptions often change as treatment progresses. Avoid finalizing new glasses or contact lens prescriptions until IOP and refraction have been stable for at least 3 months following intervention.

simpleName: Primary Ocular Hypotony (unexplained low eye pressure)
metaTitle: Primary Ocular Hypotony: Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Primary ocular hypotony: unexplained intraocular pressure of 5 mmHg or below. Symptoms, workup, atropine, midodrine, and surgical care reviewed.
